data.title: Eurozone Industrial Outlook Thrown Into Uncertainty As Energy Woes Return
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

While the European Commission is making progress in implementing the Industrial Accelerator Act, the conflict in the Middle East is currently clearly serving as an industrial decelerator, as higher energy prices and broader supply chain disruptions are putting the brakes on the much-anticipated manufacturing recovery.

And today’s release of January production data already shows that recent optimism among manufacturers has not exactly been justified by production data. The decline of -1.5% in January follows a drop of -0.6% in December. While production had been elevated compared to 2024 levels for much of last year, the January level of industrial production is now the lowest since December 2024.

It has to be said that a large drop in Irish production, whose data is notoriously volatile, contributed significantly to the decline in January, but the misery was shared more broadly as Germany, Italy and Spain all noted declines compared to December.

Stronger production hopes hinge on the expectation that increased public investment in defence and infrastructure will boost manufacturing output. But the risk is that another prolonged surge in energy costs will shatter hopes of a recovery in energy-intensive industries, which have already been struggling since the previous shock in energy prices in 2021-22. While other sectors have managed to thrive despite the headwinds from the previous shock, this could still be enough of a drag on manufacturing to pour cold water on hopes of a manufacturing recovery.

So just as optimism had returned to manufacturing, geopolitics is clearly putting downside risk back on the table.

data.title: BGC Group: FMX Expansion Is Transforming The Business, But Expectations Are Already High
data.description:
My thesis

In my view, the current valuation suggests that investors are already pricing in significant success of the FMX platform and the company’s strategic transformation. BGC Group, Inc. (BGC) ended the 2025 financial year having reached record revenues of $2.94 billion, which is a 30% growth compared to last year. This increase happened because of organic 14.9% revenue growth across all asset classes and OTC Global Holdings integration, after which the company became the world's largest energy broker.

The biggest part of the strategy still remains the FMX Futures Exchange platform, which at the end of 2025 captured 40% of the market share of US treasury bond net transactions. Fenics also continues to increase the efficiency of its activities. The company's overall debt is reaching $1.2 billion, while the cash reserve makes up $851.5 million.

The current P/E rate reaching around 29x suggests that the market's valuation is based on high expectations for FMX's ability to take away liquidity from CME Group. Investors have to assess the risk that in 2026 the planned $25 million cost savings could be absorbed by the continued technological investments, which are mandatory to fight competitors in the futures market, so the 21.3% adjusted profit margin remains the main stability indicator.

Business overview

BGC Group is one of the biggest wholesale financial market intermediaries in the world, providing services to institutional clients, such as investment banks, risk insurance funds, and governments. The company operates as a bridge between the buyers and the sellers in interest rate, foreign exchange, credit, equity, and commodity markets.

In 2025, the company reached record numbers financially, where yearly revenues grew 30% and reached $2.94 billion. A significant boost was given by the energy, raw materials, and shipping (ECS) segment, which, after the successful acquisition of OTC and its integration, became the largest energy broker in the world, generating 33% of the total revenue.

The business is diversified, though the main business activity is focused on the EMEA region, making 53% of the revenue, and also in the US market with a 36% share. The business model is based on commissions for executing transactions, and the efficiency of the activities is measured by the income generated per broker, which in 2025 reached $1.18 million. The company's market capitalization currently reaches $4.3 billion, while the P/E ratio is around 29x, reflecting the market's expectations for further technological expansion and the market share growth in the treasury securities segment.

Strategic shift

BGC Group is carrying out a strategic shift, reshaping the traditional voice mediation model into a highly profitable electronic platform managed by the Fenics brand. This change is the main factor for the margin expansion, because electronic trading platform revenue at the end of 2025 grew 15.4%, well over the overall market average.

The most significant rotation element is FMX Futures’ exchange activity, which directly competes with CME Group in the U.S. Treasury bond and SOFR futures markets. At the end of 2025, FMX UST platform occupied a record 40% net treasury bond market share, while SOFR futures contract volume in the last quarter jumped 82%.

This migration to fully electronic systems allows the company to implement a cost reduction program, whose goal is to save $25 million over the year in 2026, decreasing dependency on the expensive broker staff. Product rotation also includes data and analytics services expansion, striving to create recurring revenue streams, which lowers business sensitivity to cyclical market activity fluctuations. The switch to integrated solutions, including cash, future trades, and derivatives in one ecosystem, positions the company as a full-fledged exchange operator, not just a traditional broker.

Valuation

BGC Group's share price is currently around $9.30, which reflects the market's valuation after the record results in 2025. The company's revenues in the last year grew 30%, which was led by the integration of OTC Global Holdings as well as the growth of the electronic trading platform Fenics.

The company's forward P/E is reaching 7-8x, though the GAAP P/E rate is over 29x, because of $54.8 million in one-off restructuring costs. The main value engine is the FMX platform, which occupied 40% of the US treasury bond market share. Even though the EV/EBITDA rate of 15.5x suggests a technological premium, the free cash flow yield is reaching 10.5%. Dividend yield remains low, 0.9%, because capital is directed to FMX infrastructure. Current valuation shows that the market recognizes the market model switch, though it remains cautious due to a possible slowdown in trading activity.

Comparison

BGC Group shares are currently traded with a forward P/E of 7-8x, which is not too far off from the main competitor, TP ICAP Group (TCAPF), with its 7.6x rate. Though the main difference comes out when valuing the P/B ratio, where BGC Group's is reaching 4.4, while TP ICAP's P/B ratio is just 1.0.

Such disproportion suggests that investors value BGC assets as way more expensive for the successful FMX platform's expansion and the bigger operational efficiency, even though both companies have a similar forward P/E. For comparison, Compagnie Financière Tradition (CFNCF) is traded with a 15.5x forward P/E and 3.7 P/B ratios, which shows the market's confidence in its more stable, but slower business model growth. Meanwhile, CME Group, which is a direct BGC competitor in the treasury bond futures market, maintains a 26.6x forward P/E, highlighting a huge gap between exchange operators and brokers.

BGC's goal of 18% ROE justifies a higher P/B ratio compared to TP ICAP, whose ROE is not even reaching 9%. These numbers confirm that BGC is successfully transforming from a pure voice intermediary to a technology financial infrastructure company, though it still isn't able to reach the valuation level of full-fledged exchanges.

Risks

BGC Group's risks currently remain directly tied to FMX's future exchange's ability to withstand CME Group's competition. Though FMX, until the end of 2025, had a 40% share in the US treasury markets, pulling liquidity into SOFR futures trades requires constant financial incentives, which limit profitability.

If trading volumes, which at the end of 2025 grew 82%, stabilize or even drop, investments into the platform will turn negative. Regulation changes in the US related to mandatory central clearing increase compliance costs and operational complexity.

The company's financial stability depends on the $25 million cost savings plan's success, because staff costs still make up around 60% of the revenues. With $1.2 billion in overall debt and a 1.34 debt-to-equity ratio, BGC Group has no room for error when the interest rates are high. Revenue concentration in the US and EMEA markets and the dependency on market fluctuations mean that the decreasing macroeconomic uncertainty will directly lower the broker's revenues. The current market's valuation has already priced in aggressive growth, so any FMX expansion lag could cause a sudden decline in the share price.

Thesis summary

BGC Group is currently experiencing a transformation period, and the company is gradually moving away from the traditional voice mediation model to the technology based infrastructure of electronic platforms. Record revenues in 2025 and strong growth show that the strategy is starting to give results, especially after OTC Global Holdings integration and the fast Fenics and FMX platform development. FMX futures market expansion is becoming the main long term engine for growth, because the company is trying to take away part of the liquidity from the market leaders, such as CME Group.

However, in my view, the current valuation already reflects the high expectations from the FMX platform's success and the further business model expansion. Though BGC Group is generating strong revenue growth and is improving business efficiency, investors need to evaluate the risk of competitiveness, large technological investments, and the possible cyclicality of trading activity in financial markets. That is why further share price dynamics will mostly depend on whether the company manages to maintain FMX's share of the market, efficiently lower costs, and further expand revenue generated by electronic platforms.

data.title: Oracle: The AI Infrastructure Juggernaut Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is in the middle of a massive bet as it's pouring capital into AI cloud infrastructure at a pace that would have been unthinkable a few years ago. ORCL’s Q3 fiscal 2026 earnings backed up the story with 22% YoY revenue growth, 84% cloud infrastructure growth, and a 325% jump in remaining performance obligations (RPO) to $553 billion. The part that has the market worried is that ORCL is borrowing heavily to reimagine the future of its company. ORCL’s long-term debt has ballooned to $124.72 billion, and it is generating -$24.74 billion in free cash flow (FCF). I’m bullish on ORCL for the long term because I think its cloud infrastructure spending will drive serious cash from operations growth over the next two to three years, just like we saw with the hyperscalers before it. This is not a set-it-and-forget-it type of investment, as its data center delivery, debt management, and RPO conversion all have to go right. If ORCL doesn’t deliver on these metrics, the stock is likely to retrace to the downside. I believe that the investment case in ORCL needs years to play out, not months. Based on what the other hyperscalers have produced, I feel that ORCL’s future earnings will be strong, and this is an investment that could generate significant alpha in the future.

Oracle Showed the Market Why Their CapEx Allocations Are the Future of Their Company

ORCL’s cloud infrastructure revenue hit $4.9 billion in Q3, which was an increase of 84% YoY. The real story here is that Oracle cloud infrastructure has accelerated its YoY growth in the 2026 fiscal year from 55% in Q1 to 68% in Q2 to 84% in Q3. In the first 9 months of the 2026 fiscal year, Oracle cloud infrastructure revenue generated $12.3 billion, which exceeded the $10.2 billion generated in the entire 2025 fiscal year. That kind of acceleration makes me believe that ORCL is making the right call on capital allocation. The key driver is AI workloads, as ORCL has discussed that demand for cloud computing for AI training and inferencing continues to grow faster than supply. ORCL has become a go-to infrastructure partner for some of the world’s biggest AI model developers because they offer GPU clusters at competitive price-performance ratios that often undercut the hyperscale incumbents. ORCL is focused on its multi-cloud database, which has seen its revenue scale 531% YoY, which is an indication that demand is exceptionally strong for its workloads that work across Azure, AWS, and Google Cloud.

ORCL’s SaaS business grew 13% YoY to $4 billion, with Fusion Cloud ERP up 17% and NetSuite Cloud ERP up 14%. ORCL continues to build more SaaS applications for its customers at a lower cost due to AI-powered code generation, which management believes will expand its addressable market while improving margins. ORCL’s Cloud Revenue, which includes infrastructure and software as a service, came in at $8.9 billion, which was up 44%, while its total revenue came in at $17.2 billion, which was up 22% YoY. ORCL’s remaining performance obligations increased 325% YoY to $553 billion. Oracle cloud infrastructure is driving the lion’s share of ORCL’s revenue growth right now. Their cloud revenue now accounts for 52% compared to 48% last quarter, and that revenue mix shift is the central thesis for Oracle bulls. ORCL is moving away from its old license/support model and into a high-growth recurring cloud revenue stream.

ORCL is playing catch-up with CapEx as they have now allocated $48.25 billion in the TTM. This is an increase of 127.43% YoY and 2,159.95% since the 2021 fiscal year. ORCL is still well behind Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) from a CapEx allocation perspective, but they are closing the distance quickly. What I look at is the correlation between CapEx allocation and cash from operations growth. It looks like MSFT, AMZN, and GOOGL have witnessed big cash from operations growth as more data centers came online to fulfill their RPO. The interesting part is that ORCL’s cash from operations growth has trailed its peers by a wide margin, and I believe it is because they were late to the game with deploying CapEx. Since 2021, AMZN has increased its cash from operations by 201.15%, while MSFT’s has grown 109.16%. ORCL, on the other hand, has increased by 48.01%. ORCL is generating $23.51 billion in cash from operations, while MSFT is producing $160.51 billion, and AMZN is generating $139.51 billion. I believe that ORCL’s cash from operations will have its own growth spurt over the next two years as they catch up on the CapEx outlay. If the hyperscalers have taught us anything, it is that the cash from operations trails CapEx investments. I think the market is missing this.

The amount of CapEx that ORCL is spending has the market torn. ORCL is spending $50 billion in planned CapEx for fiscal year 2026 compared to its operating cash flow of $23.51 billion. They’re burning more than double what they bring in, and their FCF is in the negative at -$24.74 billion. Unlike MSFT or AMZN, ORCL can’t just absorb that hit because its cash generation is a fraction of what they're able to produce. ORCL’s entire financial picture depends on this CapEx actually turning into revenue on or ahead of schedule so they can monetize their RPO. If there are delays getting data centers online and generating returns, the pressure on the balance sheet will intensify as the carrying costs on their debt continue to increase. I’m comfortable with the risk because I think the payoff is coming, but the reality is that this adds a layer of volatility that investors need to size appropriately.

ORCL’s Debt Load is the Elephant in the Room

I think ORCL’s debt is the single biggest variable in its investment case. ORCL has constructed a plan to raise between $45 and $50 billion during 2026, which will come from debt and equity. On the debt side, they went to market with a $25 billion bond offering in early February. It was an eight-part deal led by Goldman Sachs, and the order book hit $129 billion. What worries me more is the rolling nature of the debt. ORCL is taking on new obligations at today’s rates while older, cheaper debt matures and eventually needs to be refinanced at higher costs. Total long-term debt jumped 24.74% in a single quarter to $124.72 billion. The debt-to-equity ratio dwarfs its hyperscale peers.

On the equity side, ORCL set up a $20 billion at-the-market program through Citigroup Inc. (C) to sell up to 121.5 million shares at market prices. That dilutes current shareholders, but it’s the trade-off management made to protect the investment-grade rating. They also said they won’t issue more bonds beyond the $50 billion amount in calendar year 2026. In order to make this seem reasonable, I believe that management was careful to highlight that for the large AI contracts, a lot of the equipment is funded through customer prepayments or customer-supplied GPUs. This is important because it takes some CapEx off ORCL’s books. In the short term, this doesn’t solve the issue of generating $23.5 billion in operating cash flow against $50 billion in CapEx. ORCL is continuously needing to raise capital, and it's getting more expensive as the interest expenses continue to expand.

Oracle’s Valuation is Very Interesting at These Levels

When I line up ORCL’s forward earnings against AMZN, GOOGL, MSFT, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), it looks cheap. ORCL is expected to generate $7.44 of EPS this year, $7.94 next year, and $10.72 the year after that. ORCL is currently trading at 21.92 times this year’s earnings and 15.22 times 2028 earnings. ORCL has 44.09% EPS growth on the horizon over the next 2 fiscal years. ORCL is trading at the 2nd lowest forward valuation out of these companies, with the third largest amount of EPS growth. From an earnings perspective, I think that ORCL looks inexpensive, especially given what the next several years of profitability could look like as they transform RPO to straight revenue. I think that the market is overlooking ORCL’s earnings potential because of its debt load, and this could be a costly mistake over the next several years.

What Management is Telling Us About the Road Ahead

The forward guidance from the Q3 call gives us a useful roadmap. For Q4 in fiscal year 2026, management expects total revenue to grow 19% to 21% in USD, with cloud revenue growing 46% to 50%. They guided non-GAAP EPS to $1.96 to $2.00. For the full fiscal year 2026, the company stuck with its $67 billion revenue target and $50 billion CapEx plan; no changes there. The big number that I am watching out for is the $90 billion of revenue that management believes they will generate next year. That’s roughly 34% growth YoY, which could become a narrative changer since ORCL is currently doing roughly $67 billion in revenue. ORCL is focusing on its future footprint to make its revenue guidance a reality. The Abilene, Texas campus is roughly a 1,000-acre site with eight data centers. Management said on the Q3 call that two buildings are already running workloads on Nvidia GB200 Blackwell racks, and the remaining buildings are scheduled for completion by mid-2026. This will bring the total capacity to about 1.2 gigawatts. In addition to this site, ORCL has committed to building 4.5 gigawatts of AI data center capacity for OpenAI across several U.S. sites, which include campuses near Detroit and in Wisconsin. In Q3, ORCL delivered more than 400 megawatts of capacity to its customers, and 90% was delivered on time or early.

ORCL’s CEO, Clay Magouyrk, discussed margins on the Q3 call, which provided some encouraging information. He indicated that the AI capacity delivered in Q3 came in at a 32% gross margin, which exceeded their guidance of 30%. He made sure to discuss the fact that as ORCL’s margins expand, its fixed costs get spread across a larger revenue base, which causes profitability to improve. It was also discussed that their multi-cloud expansion will have 33 live regions with MSFT, 14 with GOOGL, and 8 with AWS, which is expected to increase to 22 by the end of Q4. This is an indication that there is strong demand for their multi-cloud. Even though management is signaling that they can generate $90 billion of revenue in 2027, the reality is that ORCL needs to keep converting RPO into revenue while delivering data centers on time. This allows them to keep their customers locked in, but if they miss this target, then I could see shares of ORCL declining as the market knows how much leverage is behind it.

Risks to Investing in ORCL

The biggest risk to ORCL is the balance sheet. ORCL has $38.46 billion in cash and another $677 million in marketable securities, but its long-term debt is $124.72 billion. That’s a net liquidity position of -$85.59 billion. Their CapEx exceeds their cash from operations by so much that FCF is -$24.74 billion. If the AI buildout stalls or demand shifts, ORCL is the most exposed name in the group because it is not funding this from profits the way its peers are. It’s important to note the debt dynamics because the $25 billion bond deal was priced at a premium to peers, and the market knows ORCL is sitting on over $100 billion in debt with negative free cash flow. As older, cheaper bonds mature, they’ll be replaced with new debt at today’s rates. That means interest expense is likely to increase even if ORCL doesn’t borrow another dollar. If ORCL’s revenue doesn’t ramp the way management expects, they may end up in a scenario where debt servicing costs eat into operating income and mitigate their flexibility.

Then there’s the RPO concentration issue, as the $553 billion in RPO is loaded with large-scale AI contracts. ORCL acknowledged that most of the Q3 RPO increase came from these deals, which is a difficult spot to be in, considering AI infrastructure is a fast-moving market and customer preferences can shift overnight. A slowdown in AI model training from efficiency improvements or a shift from training to inference workloads could create problems for Oracle Cloud infrastructure. Recently, OpenAI walked away from 600 megawatts of Abilene expansion because it wanted newer chips. That’s exactly the kind of thing that can happen when you’re building physical infrastructure for a market that moves quickly. ORCL’s whole plan is to grow its way out of this debt, and if RPO conversion slows or contracts get restructured, the math behind ORCL’s profit model gets ugly quickly.

The future buildout has its own set of problems that ORCL doesn’t have control over. We have already seen labor shortages and material constraints cause delays. Power availability is a bottleneck everywhere in the data center industry right now, and if they can’t secure access to continuous power, their data center plans become worthless. GPU generations are turning over faster than buildings can be constructed, so there’s a real risk that by the time a facility is done, the hardware may not meet the customers' demands. If ORCL can’t deliver data centers on time, the carrying costs will negatively impact the top and bottom lines, and that will impact the balance sheet even more.

Conclusion

ORCL’s Q3 was a strong quarter as RPO increased 325%, cloud infrastructure revenue accelerated to 84% YoY, and management guided for $90 billion in revenue for next year. ORCL is putting itself right in the center of the AI infrastructure race, and I think the market is underpricing what that could be worth over the next several years. Based on today’s forward earnings, ORCL looks cheap, but it's priced this way for a very good reason. ORCL has $124.72 billion in debt, $50 billion in new capital raises, and a balance sheet that is stretched unlike its peers. If ORCL can deliver on its projections, its share price could rip higher, but if something goes wrong, such as construction delays or a pullback in AI spending, then we could see ORCL experience a credit downgrade. This is a high-risk, high-reward scenario because there is so much leverage on ORCL’s balance sheet. I’m bullish on ORCL here because I believe the amount they can scale their cash from operations over the next several years will make up for the large-scale buildout that the market is worried about.

data.title: The Swiss Army Knife Approach To Retirement Success
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

In the late 1880s, the Swiss Army purchased a folding pocketknife for their soldiers to open canned food and for the maintenance of their service rifle, which required a screwdriver for assembly and disassembly. In 1891, a Swiss manufacturer engineered a multi-tool knife that held multiple tools on a single spring mechanism. This gave birth to one of the most useful and versatile multi-tools in the history of humankind—the Swiss Army Knife.

The Swiss Army Knife was never meant to be the best knife in the world, nor was it designed to replace a full workshop. It is compact, fits in your pocket, and doesn’t look like much, but it is indispensable when you really need it. Its brilliance lies in preparedness, being ready for most situations without unnecessary complexity.

Income investing works in the same way.

Many investors attempt to build precision instruments for every possible scenario: one allocation for growth, another for inflation, another for evolving interest rate cycles, another for defense against inflation. In the pursuit of participating in every evolving scenario, portfolios often become complex, fragile, and emotionally exhausting to manage

Consider approaching your portfolio like a Swiss Army knife.

Instead of chasing the perfect instrument for every market condition, you can focus on versatile cash-flow generators, with assets that can function across cycles. Not necessarily the biggest beneficiaries of a new trend, or the fastest growers, or the highest yielders, but those that are durable, adaptable, and useful to hold across market conditions.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we have several tools in our income arsenal, and our diversified approach is called the Income Method. It is much like the Swiss Army Knife, it may not be the only path to financial independence, but it is a versatile and resilient one, designed to help investors stay prepared for whatever comes next. Without further ado, let us look at the top tools in our Swiss Army Knife portfolio.

Pick #1: UTF – Yield 7.2%

When we last discussed Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), we discussed the opportunity in its usual discount to NAV amidst soaring demand for electricity and wrote:

We invest to be cash collectors, to grab our cut of all that spending, and the utility sector is actively securing multi-year contracts from these high-flying tech firms to support their AI conquest ambitions. When and whether AI spending will reflect on the bottom lines of these firms, and by how much, remains to be seen. But by maintaining a diversified exposure to leading utility firms, you can collect steady income today. – HDO Article from October

It has been a terrific ride since, leaving the AI-heavy S&P 500 in the dust in price and total returns.

In our Market Outlooks written for HDO subscribers, we have consistently outlined the massive disconnect between the value and growth category valuations. In early 2026, we have seen value stocks outperform, and utilities have participated in the rally, but there is much more where that came from. Source

The markets are asking, “Where is the money?” and boring utilities have the answer. Trading at a ~9.5% discount to NAV, UTF offers compelling value, providing discounted access to a cash-flow-rich sector. To put things visually, we have not seen such a discount from UTF in many years.

UTF has 301 holdings, with its top 10 representing ~29% of the CEF’s invested assets. These are some of the largest, diversified, and most regulated utility firms in North America. Source

UTF doesn’t invest only in utility common stock. In fact, electric utility only represents 36% of the CEF’s assets, while gas distribution stands at 7%. UTF maintains significant exposure to midstream firms (14%) and towers (5%), which are strong cash-receiving participants in the data center era, and the CEF also has ~18% exposure to corporate bonds and preferreds, providing much-needed balance and stability.

About 60% of UTF’s portfolio is invested in companies with investment-grade balance sheets, providing a higher degree of safety and stability in this market that is increasingly concerned about credit risk.

The CEF’s 2025 distributions were funded from Net Investment Income and Realized Capital Gains, and its current monthly $0.1550/share distribution calculates to a 7.2% yield

Pick #2: PFFA – Yield 9.7%

Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) is our favorite fund for preferred securities. This ETF is actively managed, and its portfolio often reflects a strong match with our own strategy at High Dividend Opportunities. Source

In Q4 2025, PFFA’s best performers were preferred securities from Babcock & Wilcox (BW), Apollo Global Management (APO), and Edison International (EIX). We share our success with two of these investments.

PFFA charges a reasonable 0.8% management fee (dividends are net of all fund expenses), and the remainder in its 2.5% expense ratio is interest expenses. The fund’s active management and tactical use of leverage have contributed to meaningful outperformance relative to more passive, index-tracking preferred ETFs. Quality comes at a price, and the investor who scoffed at the expense ratio and picked PFF instead would have accepted materially lower total returns over the same period. The headline expense ratio alone tells you very little. It’s like choosing Taco Bell over a high-end Mexican restaurant and bragging about paying less. Yes, you paid less, but the experience and overall quality are not comparable.

As of Dec. 31, 2025, financials represented the largest proportion of PFFA’s portfolio (33%), followed by mortgage REITs (20%), and real estate firms (19%). Combined, these sectors constitute over 70% of the fund, highlighting strategic allocation into preferreds from sectors that have recently been the largest issuers at above-historic average coupons.

PFFA delivers monthly dividends to shareholders, with consistent annual payout raises in the past five years. The ETF’s current $0.1725/share calculates to a 9.7% annualized yield.

Looking ahead, we expect 2026 to be a year of declining rates, and PFFA is well-positioned to harvest gains and support healthy monthly distributions while experiencing lower borrowing costs. This makes a strong case for continued raises to our monthly income from this best-in-class preferred stock ETF.

Conclusion

The Swiss Army knife wasn’t built for elegance; it was built for utility. Similarly, our Income Method doesn’t focus on maintaining a glowing portfolio of the best or most exciting stocks in the world. It’s built to work for us round the clock, generating cash that we can use as we see fit.

We are building a collection of durable income producers designed to work for us around the clock, steadily generating cash that we can reinvest, redeploy, or spend as we see fit.

Markets will cycle. Interest rates will rise and fall. Oil prices will swing. Headlines will alternate between optimism and fear. Utility does not panic; it functions. As we navigate another period of volatility and uncertainty, we sleep well at night knowing our next paycheck is right around the corner.

This is the power of our Income Method, and the beauty of income investing.

Performance Review

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 5.09%, topping the 2.66% advance of the benchmark S&P 500® index.

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500® index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and two interest-rate reductions by the Federal Reserve. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large- caps had only a slight advantage over small-caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

During the quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks were up 9%. All other groups lagged the index. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%), small components of the S&P 500, each lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but weakness among insurers (-1%), and energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Turning to the fund, stock selection was the primary driver of the fund's excess performance over the benchmark, especially in information technology, industrials and communication services. Overweight exposure to Alphabet contributed most at the stock level. The shares rose about 29% the past three months, as the parent of the Google search engine continued to execute on its artificial intelligence strategy while producing solid financial results across its other businesses. Investors were especially positive on the firm's fast-growing cloud-computing division, while the advertising and search segments also showed strength. In November, Google debuted an updated version of its AI model, Gemini, that company executives said represented a "massive jump" in reasoning and coding ability. We added a bit to this position, given the strong fundamental backdrop.

An out-of-benchmark stake in South Korea-headquartered SK Hynix was also among the fund's top relative contributors. The stock gained roughly 83% the past three months, rallying amid strong demand for its digital memory solutions. In a third-quarter conference call with analysts, the company said that all of its memory products – including DRAM, NAND and high-bandwidth memory chips – were sold out through 2026. To address the shortage, management said it would increase capital spending to meet demand, prioritizing technology migration and infrastructure build-out. We increased this position in view of the extraordinarily robust demand for the firm's products.

Conversely, an underweight in health care detracted in a quarter where that sector led all others. In stock-specific terms, overweighting Paycom Software (-23%) detracted most. The company offers a cloud-based human resources platform targeted to U.S. small and mid-sized companies. The stock declined partly due to broader weakness in software stocks amid concern that AI might usurp much of the functionality of software applications at a lower cost. Additionally, the company's Q3 financial results were mixed. While revenue grew by 9% year over year, earnings growth decelerated amid weaker operating margins. Several analyst downgrades also weighed on the shares. We reduced this position but maintained an overweight, given our favorable long-term view of the company.

A sizable underweight in Apple also hurt, as the stock advanced about 7% this quarter. The stock was bolstered by strong results for the quarter ending September 27. Revenue was up 8%, while earnings per share rose 13%, both surpassing consensus expectations, buoyed by robust demand for the new iPhone 17 lineup, as well as record sales in Apple's services segment. In his October 30 update, CEO Tim Cook projected growth of 10% to 12% for the holiday-season quarter. We continued to see better opportunities elsewhere.■

Outlook and Positioning

We remain optimistic about the prospects for generative artificial intelligence. We continue to believe the current breakthroughs in large language and other transformer and diffusion-based models will have massive implications for how developed economies function. The impact will be at least as big as the development of the transistor or the World Wide Web, in our opinion. Among companies positioned to benefit from AI, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Alphabet were some of the fund's largest overweights as of December 31.

Within the cyclically driven earnings bucket, a key theme for the fund is strengthening commercial aviation, which is one of the very few end markets that has not yet recovered to its pre-pandemic levels of production, despite a robust recovery in air travel. A key holding here is Boeing (BA), the fund's No. 1 overweight at year-end. The company is still working to resolve some longstanding production issues. With that said, Boeing's backlog was down to 10.6 years at the end of 2025, from 17.2 years in 2024, when its delivery numbers were very low. Additionally, the firm raised roughly $24 billion of capital in the fall of 2024 by selling additional shares of stock, helping to strengthen its balance sheet. All told, we're anticipating better times for Boeing.

Elsewhere in the industrials sector, GE Vernova (GEV) was another meaningful overweight at year-end. The company is a global leader in the electric power industry. Its largest division makes gas turbines, which are used to generate electricity. Amid the global push toward electrification and decarbonization – including supplying the world's increased power needs due to the advent of generative AI – Vernova appears well positioned.

The energy sector was a significant overweight as of year-end. The rationale here is reasonable valuations, corporate policies about returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks, and the steady albeit slow growth in global energy consumption. In this sector, the fund is focused on mission-critical infrastructure providers and exploration & production firms with low decline rates and predictable capital spending policies. Individual energy positions of note at quarter end included Enterprise Products Partners (EPD).

Information technology was our largest sector underweight at the end of Q4. Within technology, the portfolio held a sizable underweight in technology hardware & equipment, mainly because of Apple, mentioned earlier. Although the stock did well the past three months, we remain cautious about the longer-term outlook for it, given ongoing tariff concerns and intense competition in the smartphone market. The fund also remained underweight the software & services segment but overweight the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry.

Elsewhere, the fund is underweight defensive, staples-like stocks, but we're watching for opportunities to lessen that underweight in this somewhat neglected area of the market.

Overall, top individual overweights at the end of December included Boeing, Alphabet and GE Vernova. Top non-benchmark positions were Taiwan Semiconductor Company, Brookfield and Allison Transmission Holdings (ALSN). The top-three underweights were Apple, Amazon.com and Tesla (TSLA).

data.title: Microsoft: Be Greedy When Others Are Fearful
data.description:
Reasons to continue buying MSFT

I have covered Microsoft (MSFT) only once in my career as a Seeking Alpha author, and it was a bullish call as of October 27. The timing of my previous call coincided with nearly peak AI optimism, so it is no wonder that as of today my bullishness did not age well. Microsoft is currently 24% cheaper than in late October 2025 despite growing financial metrics and expanding AI foothold. The market is currently in extreme fear mode, which means that the stock price might continue stagnating for a while. However, I believe it is a great opportunity to buy the dip because "be greedy when others are fearful" usually works well when we speak about high-quality companies.

Let's start with the company's valuation. Microsoft is currently way cheaper than its historical multiples across the board. Moreover, the market prices factor in quite low premiums for Microsoft in terms of the P/E ratio as well as the price/cash flow. A narrow premium compared to the sector could have been justified if Microsoft demonstrated revenue growth and profitability in line with peers. However, this is not applicable because Microsoft's revenue is expected to grow faster than the sector by 50% in the current fiscal year, while its profitability is miles ahead of the sector.

Deep discounts compared to the company's own historical averages could have been justified if Microsoft's revenue and EPS growth were decelerating rapidly. However, this is also not true because the company's revenue growth is expected to remain within the same range of approximately 15%-16% at least until the end of the 2020s. The bottom line's CAGR is expected to be slightly higher than the revenue growth rate, which indicates that Wall Street expects Microsoft to maintain its solid operating leverage.

Thus, Microsoft's multiples should return closer to historical averages once the market sentiment improves to at least neutral or even moderately optimistic. As a result, the fair share price is the historical forward P/E of 31.6 multiplied by the current fiscal year's EPS forecast of $16.73. The product of this multiplication is nearly $530, indicating that Microsoft is currently undervalued by more than 30%.

I have high conviction in Wall Street's revenue and growth EPS estimates for Microsoft because of several qualitative factors. Microsoft's Azure still commands a massive 20%+ market share in the accelerating cloud infrastructure market, which makes it a cornerstone of the AI era. Having such a strong market share means that Microsoft's cloud business has a huge customer base, and developing new AI capabilities via the Copilot product helps the company to expand its cross-selling potential. For instance, the company recently introduced a $99/month AI-powered software suite to boost adoption of Copilot. Analysts from Morgan Stanley believe that this upgrade significantly boosts Microsoft's foothold in the AI era.

Besides the increasing potential for cross-selling, Microsoft is also entering entirely new AI-related verticals. The latest reports suggest that Microsoft is expanding its AI capabilities into the healthcare vertical with the introduction of Copilot Health. Based on what the report describes, it will be a comprehensive ecosystem bridging together healthcare (records, visit summaries, medication lists, test results) and fitness/wellness (activity levels, sleep patterns, records from smart devices like Oura or Fitbit). Such an offering looks very promising considering the secular trend of people becoming more health conscious after the COVID-19 pandemic. This strategic move also aligns well with the regulatory environment, as the U.S. government is striving to make the country's healthcare more digitalized. Overall, the global healthcare market is worth almost $9 trillion, while its digitalization is still quite low across the world. With its strong positioning in cloud infrastructure plus AI, vast expertise in digitalization, and capacity to invest in innovation, Microsoft is well-positioned to capitalize on this new healthcare-related vertical.

Finally, the technical analysis suggests that all the pessimism is already factored in by the market. The share price has been consolidated around $400 for more than a month, meaning that peak pressure from bears is likely in the rearview mirror. The last time MSFT was consolidating around $400 was in spring 2025, after which a swift rally to $500 happened just within 4 months. Considering Microsoft's strong financial performance and vital bullish qualitative developments, I think that the same calendar Q2 rally can happen again.

Reasons to avoid MSFT

However, there is one big difference in spring 2026 compared to spring 2025. There is currently a large military operation in the Middle East, which pushes energy prices much higher. In such an environment of elevated inflation risks for the U.S. economy, the Fed might switch back to a hawkish stance. A tighter monetary policy is usually bad for valuations of growth stocks because higher interest rates undermine the discounted value of future cash flow. President Trump does not expect the military operation to last long, meaning that the current spike in energy prices is likely to be unsustainable. However, it is important to understand that Iran is a large country with a quite powerful military. We already saw multiple times from the news that Iran is ready to strike the energy infrastructure of neighboring countries in the Gulf, which might disrupt global energy production or exports. Higher energy prices for longer will almost inevitably lead to a more hawkish Fed because the U.S. labor market is still quite strong. Since the labor market and inflation are the two key metrics for the Fed under its dual mandate, a relatively low unemployment rate means that there is some room for interest rate hikes.

Besides geopolitics and interest rates, there is a large business-specific risk for Microsoft. Microsoft is substantially ahead of Google in terms of cloud infrastructure market share. However, the search giant is also a relentless innovator, and its Gemini 3.1 LLM is really successful and is rapidly gaining popularity. Google also has vast financial resources to invest in R&D and M&A, which is a notable fundamental threat for Microsoft. Recent publications from BNP Paribas suggest that ChatGPT is losing its market share not only to Gemini but to Anthropic's Claude as well. While Microsoft's Copilot has already shifted from "ChatGPT-only" to a multi-LLM setup, it appears that the "Microsoft+OpenAI" narrative is still very strong for the market. Therefore, if OpenAI continues losing its edge in the LLM space, it might weigh on investor sentiment around MSFT.

The verdict

Microsoft looks too cheap to ignore, especially considering its rapidly strengthening foothold in AI and stellar financial performance. The current share price of $400 looks like an extremely strong support level, meaning that downside risks are likely low. Due to soaring energy prices, some near-term volatility might persist. However, as a long-term investor, I prefer to rely more on fundamentals than on sentiment. My rating for MSFT remains the same, a Strong Buy.

data.title: Corteva Splitting Up: One Preferred Keeps Its Strong Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

While I really dislike investing in uncertainty, and Corteva, Inc. (CTVA) having announced they are dividing into two companies definitely qualifies. I reviewed both of its preferred stocks last fall, as owning them would diversify a portfolio’s allocation away from financial issuers. Covered again here will be:

  • Corteva, Inc., $3.50 Series Cumulative Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.A).
  • Corteva, Inc., $4.50 Series Cumulative Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.B).

I contacted the company and was told the preferred stocks will be the responsibility of the new Corteva company, not the spinoff part. That also aligns with the fact that the old DuPont company was the original issuer of both preferred stocks. As then, and despite not being able to analyze the financial statements of the part retaining ownership, I’m maintaining the Strong Buy rating on CTA-B and lowering CTA-A to a Buy based on the current yield spread between the issues.

Corteva reviewed

Seeking Alpha describes this current company as (edited):

Corteva, Inc. operates in the agriculture business. It operates through two segments, Seed and Crop Protection. The Seed segment develops and supplies advanced germplasm and traits that produce optimum yield for farms. It offers trait technologies that enhance resistance to weather, disease, insects, and herbicides used to control weeds, as well as food and nutritional characteristics.

The Crop Protection segment offers products that protect against weeds, insects and other pests, and diseases, as well as enhances crop health above and below ground through nitrogen management and seed-applied technologies.

The company operates in the United States, Canada, Latin America, the Asia Pacific, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Corteva, Inc. was incorporated in 2018 and is headquartered in Indianapolis, Indiana.

Breakup news

From the news release announcing they are dividing breakup:

Indianapolis, Ind., October 1 – Global agriculture technology leader Corteva announced today that its Board of Directors has unanimously approved a plan to separate the company into two independent, publicly traded companies, one comprising its current Crop Protection business (“New Corteva”) and the other comprising its current Seed business (“SpinCo”).

  • New Corteva to be a market leader differentiated by its innovative solutions, including biologicals, and focus on operational excellence
  • SpinCo to be a leader in advanced genetics and build upon a century-long track record of customer loyalty, elite breeding, market leadership and ﬁnancial strength
  • Tax-free transaction expected to be completed in the second half of 2026

Source: Corteva: press release.

A recent announcement from Corteva said the split is now targeted for the last quarter of this year. Form 10 will be filed with the SEC in the first half of this year, which provides the pro-forma statements for both parts.

First are the Seed results for the past two years.

Now for Crop protection results.

The part staying as Corteva will have 44% of the estimated sales of the existing company, with the spinoff getting 56%. According to this article, the pro forma statements won’t be released until 2026. Due to concerns that the parts staying with the new Corteva are the slower growing businesses, Moody’s downgraded the debt from A3 to Baa1.

Even assuming only 44% of the current Total Common Equity ($25.9B) remains, covering the small amount of preferreds outstanding, even after paying off the LT debt ($1.7B), should not be a concern.

Here is an overview of the parts staying with the new Corteva. I counted that they sell these products and services in over 70 countries on six continents. Of course, that might drop after the spinoff.

Next, we see the breakdown and results by region.

Currently, international sales account for 63% of the total, with Asia's small exposure providing a potential source for major future growth. That said, unless things change, Corteva appears to be in a slow-growing business based on recent organic growth percentages. Another Seeking Alpha contributor gave his spin on what the post-split companies should look like and their investment thesis for both parts (article link).
